advantages: Nonlinear decision boundaries without hand-engineering high-dimensional feature expansions.
The kernel trick keeps memory and compute manageable when φ would be huge or infinite (e.g. RBF).
Strong performance on small-to-medium structured datasets with well-tuned RBF or polynomial kernels.
Unified framework: many algorithms (SVM, kernel PCA, GPs) share the same K abstraction.
Convex optimization for kernel SVM (in the dual) avoids local minima from neural training.
disadvantages: Training and prediction cost scale with dataset size — Gram matrix is O(n²) in memory for naive solvers.
Hyperparameters (γ, degree, C) are sensitive; poor tuning causes overfitting or underfitting.
Less interpretable than linear models — implicit φ hides which features drive decisions.
Struggle to match deep nets on large raw inputs (images, long text) where learned φ dominates.
Custom similarity functions may violate Mercer's condition and break underlying optimization.
